dimensions.name: Requirements Met
dimensions.gaps_to_10: The fix is purely infrastructure (npm install replacing a stub node_modules). All 7 task requirements are satisfied per the plan's actual_results: build exits 0, all 4 required routes return HTTP 200, app title confirmed as LittleBridge, onboarding flow verified end-to-end, home screen shows activity buttons, and /parent shows PIN gate. The minor gap is that onboarding PIN confirmation step (the 'confirm PIN' re-entry UX) is not implemented in StepPin — users enter the PIN once and it is accepted, rather than requiring two matching entries. This is a pre-existing design simplification, not introduced by this task.

dimensions.name: Error Handling
dimensions.gaps_to_10: All critical async paths have try/catch with user-visible states: ExploreCards shows 'No words found', ListenFindPage shows an error screen with Go Home button, ParentPage logs PIN errors, ParentDashboard shows a red error message. One minor gap: in OnboardingPage.handleDone(), if the IndexedDB putSetting calls fail, the error is only console.error'd — the user gets no visual feedback that their onboarding data failed to save, and the router.replace('/') is never called, leaving them stuck on the handoff step with no indication of the problem.

dimensions.name: UX Completeness
dimensions.gaps_to_10: Loading states exist on all async pages (panda spinner). Empty states exist for ExploreCards and ListenFind. All navigation links are functional. The PIN confirmation step in onboarding is absent (user sets PIN once, no re-entry to confirm) — for a children's app where the parent sets a PIN while handing off the device, a silent PIN entry with no confirmation is a usability gap but not a crash. Activity buttons on the home screen are tappable Links.

dimensions.name: Code Quality
dimensions.gaps_to_10: All source components are under 200 lines (largest is GuidedSession.tsx at 187 lines). The db.ts module is a clean data access layer. The `db as never` type cast pattern is repeated throughout the codebase — it is a workaround for a TypeScript strictness issue and constitutes a minor code smell. The SALT constant in crypto.ts is hardcoded ('littlebridge-2026') which is fine for a local PIN hash used for child-protection (not cryptographic secrets), but worth documenting.

dimensions.name: Test Coverage
dimensions.gaps_to_10: 207 unit tests across 25 suites — above average coverage. Tests cover happy paths, error scenarios (empty states, DB failures), and edge cases. E2E Playwright specs exist for onboarding, session, parent dashboard, and viewport. The primary gap is that the core fix in this task (removal and reinstallation of node_modules) cannot be covered by unit tests, and no CI step enforces that npm install is run before build in the worktree environment.

dimensions.name: Security
dimensions.gaps_to_10: No hardcoded API keys found in source files. No .env files present. Parent PIN is stored as SHA-256 hash with a static salt via Web Crypto API. Parent dashboard is gated behind sessionStorage 'parentAuthed' flag verified by useParentAuth hook. User-supplied child name is stored in IndexedDB and rendered via React (XSS-safe). No LLM prompt injection vectors present. The openai devDependency is only for asset generation scripts, not runtime. Minor gap: the static SALT 'littlebridge-2026' means rainbow table attacks on the 4-digit PIN space (10,000 combinations) are trivial, but this is a low-risk children's app PIN gate, not authentication for sensitive data.

issues.description: In handleDone(), if the Promise.all of putSetting calls rejects, the error is caught and console.error'd but no user-facing error message is shown and the router.replace('/') is never called. The user is stranded on the handoff step (StepHandoff) with no feedback and no way to proceed other than refreshing the page.
issues.fix_guidance: The correct approach is to add a visible error state and retry button. Add an `error` useState, set it to true in the catch block, and render an error message with a 'Try Again' button in the handoff step or in the parent OnboardingPage. Example: catch(err) { console.error(...); setError('Failed to save. Please try again.'); }

issues.description: The PIN entry step does not ask the user to confirm the PIN by entering it twice. A parent setting a 4-digit PIN on behalf of a child could easily mistype and be locked out. This is a UX gap for a parental-control feature.
issues.fix_guidance: The correct approach is a two-phase PIN entry: first entry stores the PIN in local state, then a second screen asks the user to re-enter it. Only if the two entries match does onNext(hash) get called. On mismatch, display an error and reset to the first entry.

issues.description: After 3 wrong PIN attempts the PinPad is disabled (pointer-events-none), but the app provides no automatic lockout timer — a user can simply refresh the page to reset the wrongAttempts counter to 0. The 'Forgot PIN' / full-reset modal is the only escape, which destructively wipes all progress. This is a pre-existing design limitation, not introduced by this task.
issues.fix_guidance: Persist the lockout state (timestamp + attempts) in IndexedDB or sessionStorage so a page refresh does not bypass it. Alternatively, implement a countdown timer before unlock. This prevents trivial bypass of the 3-attempt limit.

issues.description: The static SALT 'littlebridge-2026' means that all installations of this app share the same salt. An attacker with access to the device's IndexedDB can precompute a rainbow table covering all 10,000 possible 4-digit PINs. This is low risk in a children's education app but worth noting.
issues.fix_guidance: The correct approach is to generate a random per-device salt when the PIN is first created, store the salt alongside the hash in IndexedDB, and use it in both hashPIN and verifyPIN. This makes precomputed rainbow tables infeasible.

summary: This PR is an infrastructure-only fix: it replaces a stub node_modules directory (248K, 2 incomplete packages) that was blocking the Next.js webpack build with a full npm install (546 packages). No source code was modified. The root cause was correctly identified and the fix is appropriate and verified: build exits 0 with 13 pages compiled, all 4 required routes return HTTP 200, and 207 unit tests pass. The existing application code is well-structured: all components are under 200 lines, IndexedDB is used for persistence (not in-memory), error states are implemented on all critical paths, and no hardcoded secrets are present. All four P2 issues identified are pre-existing limitations that were not introduced by this task. The average dimension score is 8.33 and all individual dimensions are 8 or above, so the verdict is pass.
